passage: Consequentialism holds that the rightness of an act depends entirely on its outcomes; utilitarianism says the right act produces the greatest balance of well-being over suffering, counting everyone's interests equally. A rival tradition locates rightness in duties and the respect owed to persons: some acts are wrong in themselves because they treat a person merely as a means to an end, regardless of the good they might produce. Critics say consequentialism can license troubling acts whenever the numbers favor them and struggles to protect the individual against the group; critics of duty ethics reply that rigid rules can produce avoidable misery. Rule consequentialism asks not which act but which general rule, if widely adopted, would produce the best outcomes.
stem: The passage is primarily concerned with:
options: contrasting two traditions about what makes an action right
proving that utilitarianism is superior to duty-based ethics
recounting the biography of an influential philosopher
showing that moral claims are nothing but personal preference
label: good

stem: According to the passage, a central objection to consequentialism is that it:
options: denies that outcomes have any moral weight
may permit sacrificing an individual when the numbers favor the group
requires no reasoning or calculation at all
is indistinguishable from duty-based ethics
label: good

stem: Rule consequentialism, as described, is best understood as an attempt to:
options: reject outcomes as a basis for morality
evaluate each individual act in isolation
ground stable duties like promise-keeping in their overall consequences
prove that duty-based ethics is incoherent
label: good

stem: The author's stance toward the two traditions is best described as:
options: even-handed, noting each is weakest where the other is strongest
openly contemptuous of both traditions
enthusiastically committed to utilitarianism
indifferent to whether morality can be reasoned about
label: bad

stem: Which claim is best supported by the passage?
options: Duty-based ethics judges acts solely by their consequences
Utilitarianism counts one person's welfare more heavily than another's
A favorable outcome cannot, for duty ethics, cancel a wrong done to a person
Both traditions agree that morality is a matter of taste
label: bad

passage: The printing press changed the scale of copying: an identical text could be produced in hundreds of copies, and identical copies made a new kind of trust possible, since a reader in one city could be confident their text matched one discussed elsewhere. Standardization had unintended consequences: errors could be compared across copies and corrected, so later editions grew more accurate rather than drifting apart. But the same technology spread error and propaganda with equal efficiency, since printers printed what sold. Historians caution against crediting the press with outcomes that depended on many forces at once; it amplified tendencies already present rather than causing them.
stem: The main idea of the passage is that the printing press:
options: single-handedly caused the religious upheavals that followed
amplified existing tendencies rather than dictating a single outcome
was used only to print scripture and science
made written error impossible to spread
label: good

stem: The passage implies that standardized, identical copies mattered mainly because they:
options: allowed errors to be compared and corrected over successive editions
guaranteed that every printed book was free of error
eliminated the market for sensational pamphlets
were cheaper than any handwritten manuscript in every case
label: good

stem: The author would most likely agree that a communication technology:
options: determines by itself what a society will become
strengthens tendencies already present in a society
is useful only for spreading reliable information
had no effect on the accuracy of texts

stem: According to the passage, printers chose what to print mainly on the basis of:
options: what would sell
official approval from authorities

the wishes of scribes
label: bad

passage: Early sociologists worried the city would dissolve community, replacing the thick, overlapping ties of the village with thin ties among strangers, trading belonging for liberty. Later research complicated this: city residents built dense networks within neighborhoods, congregations, and trades, choosing communities of affinity rather than being born into them. But communities of choice can be narrow, reinforcing members' existing views and offering little contact with people unlike themselves. Some scholars argue the health of urban community depends less on how many friends a person has than on how often a city's design forces unlike people to share space.
stem: The passage is primarily concerned with:
options: proving that cities destroy all forms of community
reconsidering whether cities dissolve or reinvent community
describing the daily routine of a single neighborhood
arguing that villages are morally superior to cities
label: good

stem: The passage suggests that a drawback of 'communities of choice' is that they:
options: force members into contact with people unlike themselves
can be narrow and reinforce members' existing views
are impossible to form in a large city
always dissolve within a single generation
label: good

stem: The scholars mentioned at the end would most likely value public parks and transit because they:
options: reduce the number of friends a person needs
make cities cheaper to govern
force unlike people to share space
eliminate communities of affinity
